basmala: In the Name of God, the Infinitely Merciful, the Most Merciful.
intro: Al-Falaq — “The Daybreak” — forms with the following sūra, an-Nās, the two Muʿawwidhatān (المُعَوِّذَتَان), “the two protectresses”. Tustarī writes that here God commands the Prophet, and through him every servant, three joined movements: al-iʿtiṣām (الاعْتِصَام), to take hold of God; al-istiʿāna (الاسْتِعَانَة), to ask Him for help; and al-faqr (الفَقْر), to show one's need. And what does it mean to show one's need? He answers: it is the substitution of one state by another — for the natural disposition, left to itself, is inert, and its life is to manifest this dependence. Such is the hidden sense of the sūra: not a charm against sorcerers, but the training of a ʿabd who lets go of his own strategy. Qushayrī draws out the point at the end: if the servant ceases to guard himself by his cunning, his force, his power, his discernment, he rests from the fatigue in which the heart wavers — and he rises soon to the state of contentment, whether his desire is fulfilled or not. His tongue does not tire of asking refuge; his heart, within, is never without surrender.

versets.trad: Say: “I take refuge with the Lord of the daybreak,
versets.commentaire: al-falaq (الفَلَق) is what cleaves open — the dawn that opens in the night, but also, as Qushayrī notes, the whole of creation, everything that springs from non-being. The Lord of the daybreak is therefore the Lord of every emergence. Tustarī adds the inward key: “The best of purifications is that the servant purify himself from the illusion of his own force (ḥawl, حَوْل) and his own power (quwwa, قُوَّة). Every word and every act unaccompanied by lā ḥawla wa-lā quwwata illā bi-Llāh (لَا حَوْلَ وَلَا قُوَّةَ إِلَّا بِاللَّه) — there is no force nor power but in God — will not have His support.” To say aʿūdhu, “I take refuge”, is already to begin to let go of what one believed to hold.

mots.def: to say, to utter, to pronounce; in the imperative: “say”, “proclaim”.
to seek protection, to take refuge with someone so as to be guarded from a danger or an evil; the very gesture of the servant who withdraws into God.
rabb: the Lord, the Master; the one who owns a thing, nourishes it, raises it and brings it to its full growth.
mots.gram: preposition bi- + noun in the genitive

mots.def: that which cleaves; the dawn that opens the night; in a wider sense, all that springs into being from nothingness — the whole of creation. From the root f-l-q, “to cleave”.

versets.trad: from the evil of what He has created,
versets.commentaire: “From the evil of all that is evil,” sums up Qushayrī: the formula is deliberately total. But evil, in Sufi speech, is not another power facing God; it is what veils the Face. Every creature, insofar as it draws to itself the attention of the heart, becomes a veil for that heart. To ask refuge against what He has created is to ask not to stop at the creature, not to place trust in it, not to let anything other than God take the place of God in the inward gaze. Creation is not evil — fixation on creation, without its Origin, is.

mots.def: preposition: from, out of; here, the idea of guarding oneself from, of protection against.
evil, harm; in the Sufi reading, what turns the heart away from God.
neuter relative pronoun: what, that which; here referring to the whole of what has been created.
khalaqa: to create, to shape, to bring into being; implicit subject: God.

versets.trad: from the evil of darkness as it gathers,
versets.commentaire: Here Tustarī opens the most precious reading of the sūra. The falling night, the gathering darkness — idhā waqab (إِذَا وَقَبَ), “when it penetrates” — names, in its inward sense, an event subtler than the night outside: “its inner meaning refers to remembrance (dhikr, ذِكْر), when regard for the self (naẓar ilā l-nafs, نَظَر إِلَى النَّفْس) enters into it, screening it from the sincerity owed to God in the pure remembrance of Him.” Darkness is not outside; it advances within the very heart that invokes — the moment when the soul begins to watch itself invoke, to taste its own piety, to take measure of its station. That gaze upon the self is the true waqb, the night that penetrates prayer where one had not seen it coming. To ask refuge against it is to ask to invoke from God toward God, with no detour through self.

mots.def: coordinating conjunction: and.
preposition: from, out of; here, the idea of protection against.
evil; here, what veils the soul in its very act of invocation.
what grows dark; first the falling night or the declining moon (according to a ḥadīth), in its inner sense the soul's gaze upon itself at the heart of prayer.
temporal particle: when, at the moment that.
waqaba: to penetrate, to slip in, to spread; in speaking of night, to advance and envelop everything — and by extension, to slip in from within.

versets.trad: from the evil of those who blow upon knots,
versets.commentaire: In their first sense, an-naffāthāt fī l-ʿuqad (النَّفَّاثَات فِي الْعُقَد) are the sorceresses who breathe a word upon a knotted cord, claiming to bind a will. In the spiritual sense, the sūra reaches something more daily: every blown word that ties. The breath (nafth, نَفْث) is the thin thread of air one expels with an intent — slander that binds another's name, judgment that fixes the other in place, self-serving prayer that tries to bend God. Each time a word leaves us in order to tie, we are blowing upon a knot. And each time a word is sent our way to bind us, we need refuge. The remedy is in upright speech — the word that unties rather than ties, because it is turned toward God and not toward the other as a means.

mots.def: coordinating conjunction: and.
preposition: from, out of; here, the idea of protection against.
evil, harm.
naffāthāt: intensive form of the verb nafatha (to blow out a word); those who blow while whispering, who bind a will by breath — in the wider sense, every blown word that ties.
preposition: in, on, upon.
ʿuqda: a knot, a binding; in the plural, the knots — such as those of a cord used in casting a spell; figuratively, what human speech tries to bind in another's will.

versets.trad: and from the evil of the envier when he envies.”
versets.commentaire: “Envy (ḥasad, حَسَد) is the worst of qualities,” writes Qushayrī, and he draws from it the point of the whole sūra: “This is the teaching of how evils are warded off by God. Whoever's reliance on God is sound, his realisation in God is sound. When the servant relies, God grants him reliance only when his state makes it possible that what he deposits in Him will suffice him. And man has need that evil be turned aside from him. If he ceases to guard himself by his own cunning, his force, his power, his understanding, his discernment, at every instant — he rests from the fatigue in which the heart wavers in strategy, and he soon rises to the state of contentment (al-riḍā, الرِّضَا), whether his desire be fulfilled or not. There is the Supreme Kingdom: outwardly his tongue never tires of asking refuge; within, his heart is never without surrender (al-taslīm, التَّسْلِيم) and contentment.” Tustarī reaches further still. On this last verse, he reports from Ibn ʿAbbās: the envier is also the lower self of man, and his own eye. The enemy to flee is not only outside. It is that avid gaze from within, that wants to possess what it has not been given — and it too must be handed over to God.

mots.def: coordinating conjunction: and.
preposition: from, out of; here, the idea of protection against.
evil, harm.
ḥāsid: the envier, the one who wishes another to lose the good he holds; according to Ibn ʿAbbās, also names the lower self (nafs) of man and his own eye.
temporal particle: when, at the moment when envy becomes an act.
ḥasada: to envy; to wish another to be deprived of a good or a grace he has received — the passion most opposed to tawakkul, which is to rest in God for what one has and for what one has not.
